modules.label: http · gateway
modules.seam: the single front door every request enters; auth + routing live behind one small handler interface
modules.iface: handle(request) → Response; validates auth token, routes to a domain service, never touches the store directly

modules.label: auth · service
modules.seam: token verification is the whole contract; callers never see the signing keys or session table
modules.iface: verify(token) → Principal | None; issue(credentials) → Token; key rotation is internal

suggestions.title: Hide key rotation behind a KeyProvider port
suggestions.problem: Key rotation logic is interleaved with verification, so a rotation bug can silently break auth for every caller.
suggestions.solution: Extract a KeyProvider port with a rotating in-memory adapter; auth-service depends on the port, not the rotation schedule.
wins: rotation testable in isolation
verify() stays a pure function of (token, keyset)
swap to an HSM adapter without touching auth

suggestions.title: Move session lookup off the hot verify() path
suggestions.problem: Every verify() hits the session table even for stateless tokens, coupling latency to store-postgres load.
suggestions.solution: Cache the principal for the token TTL behind the existing verify() interface; the store becomes a cold-path fallback.
wins: p99 verify latency drops
store load no longer scales with request rate

suggestions.title: Collapse issue() and refresh() into one mint path
suggestions.problem: issue() and refresh() duplicate claim-assembly logic that has already drifted once.
suggestions.solution: Route both through a single mint(claims) helper; the two public entry points just build claims.
wins: one place to change token shape
drift between issue and refresh becomes impossible

suggestions.title: Pin password hashing to argon2id at the seam
suggestions.problem: The hashing scheme was implicit in issue(), making an upgrade a cross-cutting change.
suggestions.solution: Introduce a Hasher port with an argon2id adapter; credentials are verified through the port.
wins: scheme is a one-line adapter swap
explicit, audited crypto boundary
suggestions.note: Adopted argon2id behind a Hasher port; legacy bcrypt verified for one release then dropped.

suggestion.title: Hide key rotation behind a KeyProvider port
suggestion.problem: Key rotation logic is interleaved with verification, so a rotation bug can silently break auth for every caller.
suggestion.solution: Extract a KeyProvider port with a rotating in-memory adapter; auth-service depends on the port, not the rotation schedule.
wins: rotation testable in isolation
verify() stays a pure function of (token, keyset)
swap to an HSM adapter without touching auth

modules.label: order · service
modules.seam: owns the order aggregate; the only writer of order rows, so invariants are enforced in one place
modules.iface: place(cart) → Order; cancel(orderId) → void; reads go through query views, writes through the aggregate

suggestions.title: Emit domain events instead of leaking SQL into the service
suggestions.problem: order-service writes directly to store-postgres in two cancel paths, leaking the schema across the seam.
suggestions.solution: Have the aggregate emit OrderPlaced / OrderCancelled events; a projection owns the SQL.
wins: the leak edge to store-postgres disappears
audit log falls out for free
schema changes stop rippling into the service

suggestion.title: Emit domain events instead of leaking SQL into the service
suggestion.problem: order-service writes directly to store-postgres in two cancel paths, leaking the schema across the seam.
suggestion.solution: Have the aggregate emit OrderPlaced / OrderCancelled events; a projection owns the SQL.
wins: the leak edge to store-postgres disappears
audit log falls out for free
schema changes stop rippling into the service

modules.label: pricing · engine
modules.seam: pure calculation; given a cart and a rule set it returns a price and never reads the world
modules.iface: price(cart, rules) → Money; deterministic, no I/O, no clock

modules.label: store · postgres
modules.seam: the persistence boundary; everything above it speaks rows, nothing above it speaks connections
modules.iface: query(sql, params) → Rows; tx(fn) → result; pool + retry handled internally

modules.label: search · indexer
modules.seam: consumes order events and maintains a read-optimized index; write path is one-directional
modules.iface: index(event) → void; search(query) → Hits; the index is rebuildable from the event log

suggestions.title: Read from an event stream port rather than polling the store
suggestions.problem: The indexer polls store-postgres on a timer, so it is both laggy and coupled to the store schema.
suggestions.solution: Subscribe to an EventStream port fed by the order aggregate's events.
wins: near-real-time index updates
no schema coupling to the store

suggestion.title: Read from an event stream port rather than polling the store
suggestion.problem: The indexer polls store-postgres on a timer, so it is both laggy and coupled to the store schema.
suggestion.solution: Subscribe to an EventStream port fed by the order aggregate's events.
wins: near-real-time index updates
no schema coupling to the store

modules.label: ledger · service
modules.seam: an append-only double-entry ledger; the intended replacement for the order-service's ad-hoc money tracking
modules.iface: post(entry) → EntryId; balance(account) → Money; entries are immutable once posted

plans.title: Introduce the double-entry ledger
plans.intent: Replace the order-service's ad-hoc money handling with an append-only double-entry ledger so every balance is reconstructable from immutable entries. The ledger becomes the system of record for money while order-service keeps owning the order lifecycle.

steps.title: Pin the ledger interface and entry invariants
steps.interface: post(entry) → EntryId; balance(account) → Money; reject any entry whose debits ≠ credits
steps.note: Interface frozen and reviewed; entry immutability is the load-bearing invariant.

steps.title: Build the postgres-backed entry store behind the ledger port
steps.interface: append(entry) → EntryId on an entries table; balance() folds entries through a materialized account view
steps.note: Append path landed; the materialized balance view is still being benchmarked.

steps.title: Route order-service money through the ledger and retire the old path
steps.interface: order-service posts a balanced entry per placement/cancel; legacy money columns become read-only then drop
steps.note: Cutover is the supersedes edge from ledger-service to order-service becoming real.
